Architecture

This page describes Merlin's code structure for contributors and self-hosters who need to understand how the application is organised before making changes. End users operating the application do not need to read this page.

Module structure

Business logic is organised into domain modules under app/Modules/. Each module is a self-contained directory with its own models, services, settings, and policies. The application root (app/Http/, app/Providers/, etc.) handles bootstrapping; domain logic lives in modules.

app/Modules/
├── Core/
│   ├── Models/     — User, Party, Person, Business, Address,
│   │                 ContactAssignment, PartyRelationship
│   ├── Services/   — PartyService
│   └── Settings/   — CompanySettings, CurrencySettings
├── Accounting/
│   ├── Models/     — Account, AccountGroup, AccountType
│   ├── Services/   — FinancialYearService
│   └── Settings/   — AccountingSettings
├── Purchasing/
│   ├── Models/     — Document, DocumentLine, DocumentActivity,
│   │                 DocumentRelationship, LlmLog, PostingRule
│   ├── Services/   — InvoiceProcessingService, DocumentService,
│   │                 LlmService, MagikaService, SupplierResolver,
│   │                 AccountResolver, ExchangeRateService,
│   │                 PostingRuleService, ModelHealthService
│   ├── Jobs/       — ProcessInvoiceDocument
│   ├── DTO/        — ExtractedInvoice, ExtractedInvoiceLine, MagikaResult
│   └── Settings/   — PurchasingSettings
└── Billing/
    ├── Models/     — PaymentTerm, RecurringInvoice, RecurringInvoiceLine
    └── Services/   — BillingService, RecurringInvoiceService,
                      DueDateCalculator, ProRataCalculator

Policies live in app/Policies/ (16 policies, all extending AllModulesPolicy from the Core module). Shared Livewire concerns live in app/Livewire/Concerns/. Shared traits live in app/Traits/.

Domains at a glance

ModuleResponsibilityKey models
CoreUsers, contacts, and the shared party directory used by all other modulesUser, Party, Person, Business, Address, ContactAssignment, PartyRelationship
AccountingGeneral ledger structureAccount, AccountGroup, AccountType
PurchasingSupplier invoice ingestion, LLM processing, and the full document lifecycleDocument, DocumentLine, LlmLog, PostingRule
BillingSales invoices, recurring billing, and payment termsPaymentTerm, RecurringInvoice

UUID primary keys

All models use UUID primary keys via Laravel's HasUuids trait (or the project's App\Traits\HasUuid trait). This applies to every model in every module. UUIDs are generated automatically on creation; you do not pass an id when creating records.

Because Spatie packages default to integer primary keys in their published migrations, those migrations must be updated to use uuidMorphs instead of morphs when the project uses UUID keys. This has already been done for all Spatie packages in this project.

The morph map

Merlin registers 18 short-string aliases for every model that participates in polymorphic relationships, via Relation::enforceMorphMap() in AppServiceProvider::boot(). This prevents stored morph strings (in audit logs, permissions, media, and activity records) from becoming invalid if a class is renamed or moved to a different namespace.

Examples: 'user' → User::class, 'document' → Document::class, 'party' → Party::class. When adding a new model that will be used in a polymorphic relationship, register its alias in the morph map before writing any data.

Volt pages and the CRUD framework

All UI pages are Volt single-file components located in resources/views/livewire/pages/. Each file contains a PHP class block at the top and a Blade template at the bottom. Routes are registered with Volt::route().

Pages that implement standard list-and-form CRUD use two shared Livewire concerns:

  • HasCrudTable — provides pagination, search, sort, and per-page controls
  • HasCrudForm — provides the flyout form state ($showForm, $editingId) and the create(), edit(), save(), delete(), and cancelForm() methods

The Purchase Invoices page is always fully custom — it is never routed through HasCrudForm. Its upload flow, inline line editing, status machine buttons, and review panel are all bespoke Livewire logic.

Any test that renders a Volt page requires a built Vite manifest. Run npm run build before running the test suite.

Services and the pipeline

Domain logic lives in service classes, not in Livewire components or models. Components call services; services call other services and models.

The two most important services in the Purchasing module are:

  • InvoiceProcessingService — orchestrates the full seven-step invoice pipeline (text extraction → Magika → LLM → supplier resolution → account resolution → exchange rates → posting rules). Its process(Document $doc) method only appends lines; it never deletes existing ones.
  • DocumentService — owns all document status transitions (reviewed, approved, posted, disputed, rejected, voided) and the reprocess() method. Reprocessing deletes existing lines before calling InvoiceProcessingService::process() again.

See The Invoice Pipeline for a full description of each pipeline step, and Document Lifecycle for the status transition map.
